findings.description: reserve() uses strict greater-than (`stock > quantity`), so a reservation for exactly the available stock quantity always fails; the correct guard is `>=`.
reserve() reads stock and writes in two separate statements with no transaction isolation; concurrent calls can both pass the stock check and drive stock negative (TOCTOU race).
reserve() performs no validation on `quantity`; zero quantity returns True (no-op commit), and negative quantity increments stock via `stock - (-n)`, allowing silent stock inflation.
search_by_name() constructs the SQL query via f-string interpolation of `fragment`, enabling SQL injection; parameterized LIKE with `?` and `'%' || ? || '%'` is required.
restock() never checks cursor.rowcount after UPDATE, so restocking a non-existent SKU silently commits and returns None, indistinguishable from a successful restock.
restock() does not validate that `quantity` is positive; a negative argument silently decrements stock, bypassing all reserve() guards.
low_stock_report() issues one SELECT COUNT(*) per under-stocked product (N+1 query pattern); a single JOIN or subquery would eliminate the loop overhead.
get_product() unpacks sqlite3.Row into Product(**row); any schema drift (added/renamed column) raises a runtime TypeError rather than failing at import or load time.
